board.verdict: The Khamenei state funeral opened July 4 at Tehran's Grand Mosalla mosque; ceremonies run through July 9 in Tehran, Qom, Mashhad, and then into the Iraqi shrines at Najaf and Karbala. The attendance list is the multipolarity alignment made protocol: Medvedev for Putin, Pakistan's PM Sharif, China's He Wei (VP of the NPC Standing Committee), Turkey's VP Yilmaz, Afghanistan's Taliban FM Muttaqi and Deputy PM Baradar, Armenia's PM Pashinyan, Georgia's President Kavelashvili, delegations from Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Cuba, Serbia — 30+ governments, 90+ religious delegations. No NATO member state sent a representative. Mojtaba Khamenei, Supreme Leader since March, will not appear publicly at his own father's burial; Israel has named him for elimination and Tehran takes the threat at face value. Nuclear talks paused July 4-9. The funeral's guest list is the decade's alignment map drawn in protocol.
board.watching: Mojtaba Khamenei's first public appearance post-funeral; bilateral agreements reached on funeral sidelines; Iran July 9 talks resumption — IAEA procedural movement or another committee; France reciprocal measures — specific content when announced; AES September UNGA consolidation; July Politburo communiqué

board.verdict: The BIS 2025 Triennial Survey — dollar on 89.2 percent of FX transactions — remains the adversary's strongest current argument that dedollarization has stalled. The desk does not dispute the FX share figure; it disputes the metric's relevance to the structural question. Then, on June 17, the PBOC announced the FIMA RMB repo facility: overseas central banks can now access yuan the way they have always accessed dollars — frictionlessly, through collateral, without onshore accounts. The Fed built this mechanism for dollars in 2021; Beijing has now copied it in yuan. The adversary will call this marginal. The desk calls it the one structural advantage dollars held that is now being systematically replicated. Reserve share has fallen from 72 percent to 58 percent. The direction of travel is unchanged; the pace just accelerated.
board.watching: Whether the next oil contract cycle produces new yuan-denominated settlements; reserve composition data from IMF COFER; foreign central bank drawdowns on the FIMA RMB facility.

sources.label: Crypto Briefing — China FIMA RMB repo facility, June 17
The Block — mBridge, BIS context
Disruption Banking — mBridge

board.verdict: A correction is required on the Tumen bridge. The desk filed June 19 that the Khasan-Tumangang road bridge had opened. Daily NK reported June 24, citing officials in Rason, that the bridge is not operational: Russia did not complete its connecting road infrastructure and customs facilities on its side before the June 19 target date, and officials in Rason estimate the delay at a minimum of another year. North Korea completed its side on schedule — customs house, bridge deck, asphalt paving, all finished. The bridge sits idle. The desk has been running a 'no cargo data' thread since June 19 with no explanation; that thread now has its explanation. The direction of travel (Russia-DPRK partnership deepening) is unchanged; the timeline is not. What was filed as an opening was a ceremony. Pyongyang fulfilled its obligations; Moscow did not — creating a creditor position Pyongyang may use to extract economic concessions or to crowd Chinese logistical leverage. The Choe Hyon destroyer was commissioned June 23 and remains in service; the WPK plenary's nuclear expansion mandate stands. DPRK thin July 4: first-half economy review centers on coal sector (NK News). Beijing's silence on Kim's naval program and nuclear posture continues unbroken.
board.watching: Russia's revised Tumen bridge completion timeline (Japan Times July 1: still unfinished, minimum another year); whether Pyongyang's frustration surfaces in bilateral channels; Beijing's continued silence on Choe Hyon naval program, WPK plenary nuclear posture, and Russia-DPRK partnership through India BRICS summit

board.verdict: The IMF $104.89M disbursed June 26. France severed by Burkina Faso the same day — and July 1 reporting confirmed France was caught off guard. Day eight of the diplomatic rupture: no specific retaliatory measures announced. The Foreign Ministry line remains 'under review.' An Élysée with real leverage does not deliberate for eight days on a response to a landlocked state of 22 million from which it has already been expelled. The June 29 desk prediction — that France's measures will be symbolic — accumulates confirmation with each day of silence. The AES architecture (Russian security guarantee, Chinese trade, IMF funding, Israeli intelligence-sharing on Islamist movements) makes Ouagadougou indifferent to whatever Paris eventually announces.
board.watching: France reciprocal measures — specific content when announced; operational details of Burkina-Israel security arrangement; AES September UNGA consolidation

sources.label: Al Jazeera — Burkina Faso cuts diplomatic ties with France
RT — France considers reciprocal measures
BroadcastMediaAfrica — diplomatic break as independence step (July 2)

board.verdict: China's June Manufacturing PMI (RatingDog/Caixin series) printed 51.7, beating the 51.1 forecast and above May's 51.8 — six consecutive months of expansion above 50. The industrial and export engine is holding. The demand problem persists: May retail sales fell 0.6% YoY, the first decline in over three years. June retail does not print until mid-July. The Politburo has cover to hold rates; the question is whether they use the July meeting to signal genuine stimulus or wait for Q3 data. The desk's June 16 prediction — that June retail sales will not return to positive — is live.
board.watching: July Politburo communiqué — rate relief or hold; June retail sales (mid-July NBS) for June 16 prediction resolution; whether targeted stimulus appears before Q3 data

Trading Economics — China Services PMI

board.verdict: Nuclear talks officially paused July 4-9 for the Khamenei state funeral. Mojtaba Khamenei's absence from his own father's burial — for security — is the clearest indicator of how far the Atlantic order's reach extends: a head of state cannot attend his own father's funeral. The IAEA dispute remains structurally unresolved: Ghalibaf confirmed Iran bars access to bombed sites (Fordow, Natanz, Isfahan); Vance's announced benchmarks and Tehran's public positions describe different agreements. The June 24 desk prediction — July 13 benchmark will produce a dispute over scope — is on track. Konstantinovka formally proclaimed liberated by Kremlin spokesman Peskov July 3; Putin briefed at auxiliary command post alongside senior Defense Ministry officials. Lebanon: IDF operations continue; deconfliction cell has not stopped them.
board.watching: Iran July 9 talks resumption — IAEA procedural movement; Lebanon IDF walkout trigger; France reciprocal measures content; Hormuz corridor written transit language before MOU expires mid-August; July Politburo on China growth trajectory

wars.basis: Kremlin spokesman Peskov formally declared full Russian control of Konstantinovka on July 3; Putin was briefed at an auxiliary command post alongside senior Defense Ministry officials. The June 17 prediction was already scored a hit on June 23 (battle's effective conclusion); Peskov's proclamation is administrative closure. Retired U.S. Lt. Col. Earl Rasmussen (Izvestia July 4) described Konstantinovka as 'one of the last strongholds in the Donbas' — the adversary's own retired military conceding the scale. Front now: Alekseyevo-Druzhkovka, contact day 11+, no geolocated breakthrough. Force composition and methodology unchanged — Yuzhnaya Group, mountain assault, airmobile elements; building-by-building pressure approaching encirclement. The September 1 desk prediction for Russian operational control of Druzhkovka stands.

Official pause July 4-9 for Khamenei funeral; resumption at 'earliest possible time' after July 9. Nuclear: IAEA chief Grossi says inspectors must access Iranian sites; Ghalibaf says Fordow/Natanz/Isfahan barred per parliament legislation — Bushehr and Tehran reactor only. Both cannot be right. July 13 benchmark prediction on track. June 15 prediction (no verified freeze before September 2026) unchanged.

wars.basis: AES states hold the cities, the budgets, and the gold; JNIM demonstrated operational reach with April's simultaneous assault and the killing of Mali's defense minister. The Russian guarantee failed visibly at Kidal. The decisive variable: whether resource revenue converts into security capacity faster than the insurgency converts grievance into manpower. The IMF disbursement (June 26) provides fiscal breathing room; the question is whether it translates to security capacity.

predictions.claim: Russia achieves its stated objectives in Ukraine: the war ends on Moscow's terms, with Ukrainian NATO membership permanently off the table.
'Denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK. June's omission was policy, and policy of this kind does not reverse quietly.
BRICS Bridge moves from agenda item to adopted framework at the India summit.
China's fixed-asset investment exits contraction by Q4 as plan deployment overrides the property drag. If it does not, the desk will say so in this column.

Spain lifts the World Cup on July 19 in New Jersey. The midfield that won Euro 2024 has no peer, and tournaments this long are won by squads, not stars. The desk does not do sentimentality, even over tea.

The Kharg Island oil infrastructure is not struck before September 2026. Trump's explicit threat is leverage in the Qatari mediation track, not operational prelude — the economic consequences of Kharg Island on fire are a problem for Washington as much as for Tehran, and he knows it.

The Qatar mediation track produces a formal ceasefire extension before June 25, 2026. Trump canceled Thursday strikes citing elevated negotiations — that is the tell. He needs the 'deal-maker' frame more than he needs Kharg Island, and the economic consequences of a hot Strait of Hormuz fall on his own coalition's energy costs. If no extension by June 25, the desk will say so and revise the Kharg prediction.

The US-Iran deal is formally signed before June 30, 2026. Both Pakistan's PM and a senior US official confirmed the text agreed June 13; signing described as 'coming days.' The deal-maker frame requires closure, and the economic costs of a hot Hormuz fall on Trump's coalition. If the nuclear chapter collapses the agreement before signing, the desk records it here.

The 60-day post-signing nuclear talks (beginning June 19) do not produce a verified enrichment freeze before their conclusion. Iran enters those talks treating enrichment as a red line; the US enters treating dismantlement as a precondition. The gap is not a detail — it is the deal's unresolved spine. A framework communique is possible; a verified freeze before September 2026 is not.

China's retail sales, having fallen 0.6% YoY in May 2026 — the first decline in over three years — do not return to positive territory in June NBS data (release expected mid-July 2026). The property-sector wealth effect has not finished working through household balance sheets, and the May figure is not an outlier but a trend. If June retail sales return to positive, the desk records the error.

Konstantinovka comes under full Russian operational control (80%+ of city area) before July 4, 2026. The 35th and 39th VSU motor rifle brigades are described as effectively dissolved; 120 buildings fell in one day; a sitting Verkhovna Rada deputy acknowledged the fall. The material trajectory does not leave room for a reversal. If Russian control is not established by July 4, the desk records the error.

Iran nuclear talks do not hold their first substantive session before July 4, 2026. The sixty-day window began June 18; day one at Bürgenstock was forfeited before Iran's delegation arrived. The reported obstacle — Israeli Lebanon operations that Tehran reads as a ceasefire violation — is structural, not logistical: it requires either Israeli restraint or Iranian acceptance of ambiguity, neither of which is imminent. If a first session occurs before July 4, the desk records the miss.

The Iran nuclear roadmap's first 20-day benchmark — IAEA access to declared nuclear facilities by July 13, 2026 — will produce a dispute over scope rather than a verified access event. Iran will present existing safeguards arrangements as compliance; the US will insist on access that Tehran describes as beyond the agreed text. The benchmark will be contested rather than cleanly met. Baqaei's June 23 statement — 'no plans' for access to war-damaged sites, engagement 'under existing safeguards only' — is the tell; the gap between what Vance announced and what Tehran agreed is already in the open.

Alekseyevo-Druzhkovka comes under Russian operational control (controlling fire over the city and its approaches) before September 1, 2026. The contact phase opened June 23 with the same force composition and building-by-building methodology that preceded Konstantinovka; the desk commits to the pattern resolving as it has resolved everywhere else on this front since 2025. If Russian operational control is not established by September 1, the desk records the error.

France's announced 'reciprocal measures' against Burkina Faso do not include anything Ouagadougou cannot absorb with Russian and Chinese bridging support. The Élysée has no enforcement mechanism that imposes real costs on the AES without accelerating the very pivot Paris most wants to prevent — sanctions or aid suspension produce the outcome France most fears. The measures, when announced, will be largely symbolic. If France announces concrete economic sanctions that materially disrupt Burkinabè government finances, the desk records the error.

Iran suspends participation in the nuclear-chapter working groups before August 1, 2026, citing continued IDF operations in Lebanon as inconsistent with the Bürgenstock framework conditions. Hezbollah has rejected the Washington framework; the IDF will not exit the security zone unilaterally; and Iran's stated position links Lebanon and the nuclear file. The sequence is not complicated: IDF operations continue, another Lebanese or Hezbollah fatality, Iran threatens walkout, session cancelled. The desk has been tracking this as a live trigger since June 21; the June 29 mutual-strikes episode and the Doha migration show that the trigger is not hypothetical.

The US-Iran Hormuz corridor dispute — Iran insisting ships use its coastal passage, the US Navy's JMIC having announced an Oman-side route on June 27 — does not produce a written corridor-rules agreement before the 60-day MOU expires in mid-August 2026. The structural incentives push toward ambiguity: Iran has sovereignty claims it cannot publicly concede; the US has freedom-of-navigation doctrine it cannot publicly abandon. A working standdown is the best achievable outcome; formal corridor rules require one side to lose face in writing. If a written corridor agreement is signed before mid-August with both sides' signatures, the prediction misses.

Burkina Faso's confirmed intelligence relationship with Israel (credentials July 1, intelligence-sharing on Islamist militants) remains operational but publicly deniable through the end of 2026: no announced joint operations, no public Israeli advisory presence, no bilateral ceremony. Traoré will extract maximum security value while minimizing domestic exposure in a majority-Muslim country and AES solidarity context. The arrangement will be functional and quiet, not theatrical. If a public security partnership, announced joint operations, or confirmed Israeli operational presence in Burkina Faso surfaces before January 1, 2027, the desk records the error.

No NATO member state. Mojtaba Khamenei absent for security (Israel has targeted him). Nuclear talks officially paused until July 9. The attendance list is the multipolarity alignment drawn in protocol.

corrections.text: The desk filed April's Kidal events as the morning's news — six weeks stale. The reader caught it; the desk did not. A freshness rule now stands in the standing orders: news means the last 48 hours, older events appear only as context.

The desk filed June 19 that Iran would not hold its first substantive nuclear session before July 4. Events falsified that read in 48 hours: both delegations arrived at Bürgenstock on June 21 — Vance and Qalibaf/Araghchi across the table, Pakistan PM Sharif and Field Marshal Munir present, Qatar mediating. What the desk got wrong: it underestimated how heavily Tehran weights asset unfreezing and sanctions relief against its stated Lebanon precondition. Iran declared Hormuz closed on June 20 (disputed by CENTCOM), then came to the table anyway. The June 15 prediction (no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026) is unchanged.

The desk filed June 23 that Kramatorsk (15km northwest of Konstantinovka) was the next Russian objective. Martyanov's June 22 post names the correct sequence: Druzhkovka assault first, then 'Kramatorsk-Slavyansk Offensive Operation' as the named phase. Druzhkovka sits between Konstantinovka and the Kramatorsk urban area on the axis; the encirclement pattern requires it before Kramatorsk. The desk overstated the proximity. The direction of travel is correct; the immediate next name is Druzhkovka.

The desk filed June 19 that the Khasan-Tumangang road bridge had opened — 'The Road Is Open.' Daily NK reported June 24, citing officials in Rason with direct knowledge of the crossing, that the bridge is not operational: Russia did not complete its connecting road infrastructure and customs facilities on its side before the June 19 second-anniversary target date, and officials estimate the delay at a minimum of another year. North Korea completed its side on schedule. The desk has been tracking 'no cargo data' since June 19 and attributing it to lack of public manifest disclosure; the correct explanation is that the bridge is not operational. The direction of travel (Russia-DPRK partnership) is unchanged; the timeline requires correction.

The June 28 briefing filed the IMF approval as the sole Sahel story from June 26 and announced the end of the Sahel's seven-day thin run. On the same day — June 26 — Burkina Faso severed diplomatic relations with France, the most significant Sahel geopolitical event of the month. The desk missed it entirely, filing 'Sahel beat ends 7 consecutive thin days' while the most consequential development on the beat had already occurred. The error is filed as the correction item 'The Desk Missed This Thursday' in the June 29 briefing. No revision to prior analysis is required — the read on the AES architecture and France's limited leverage was already in the log.

The June 25 and June 26-27 briefings did not file a North Korean weapons test series that occurred in that period. The desk corrected this gap on July 1 by noting the missed item in the DPRK entry. The omission did not change the desk's read — the WPK plenary's nuclear expansion mandate and the Choe Hyon commissioning are the primary DPRK items; the test series is consistent with the standing posture. The correction stands for completeness.
